content:

Introduction to Mohs Micrographic Surgery

Emerging therapies for Mohs Micrographic Surgery target specific pathophysiological mechanisms.

  • Highest cure rate among skin cancer treatments, exceeding 99% for most common skin cancers
  • Removal of tissue occurs in thin horizontal layers rather than vertical sections
  • Real-time microscopic examination during the procedure
  • Systematic mapping of cancer margins
  • Immediate reconstruction after cancer removal

BCC and Mohs Surgery

Advanced treatment options for Understanding Mohs Surgery for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) address refractory cases and improve patient satisfaction.

  • Nodular BCC: most common, generally has the best prognosis
  • Superficial BCC: limited to superficial dermis, favorable prognosis
  • Morpheaform/infiltrative BCC: aggressive growth pattern, often requires Mohs surgery
  • Basosquamous: mixed features, higher recurrence rates

title: Understanding Scar Management After Mohs Surgery: Recognition, Diagnosis, and Care

Understanding Post-Surgical Scarring

First-line therapies for Understanding Scar Management After Mohs Surgery have established efficacy and favorable safety profiles.

  • Wound tension: High tension increases scar visibility
  • Wound location: Scars on the face heal better than those on the chest or shoulders
  • Patient age: Younger patients tend to form more hypertrophic scars
  • Skin type: Darker skin types are prone to hypertrophic and keloid scars
  • Wound care: Poor care increases infection risk and scar quality
